perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means technical and non-technical information, data, software ,records, customer information, financial information, practices, processes, methods, techniques, trade secrets, products, and/or research and any other item that (a) is of a confidential nature, (b)would reasonably be considered as confidential, (c) is clearly and conspicuously marked as confidential or (d) is identified orally by the disclosing Party as confid ential. Confidential Information shall be deemed to include all notes, analyses, loan documents, compilati ons and other material prepared by a recipient of Confidential Information containing or based in whole or in part on any Confidential Information. Confidential Information does not include information or materials that (i) are or became generally known or available to the public through no fault of Receiving Part y; (ii) were already known to Receiving Party without restriction, prior to receipt from Disclosing Party, as evidenced by files in existence at the time of such disclosure; (iii) are la wfully disclosed to Receiving Party by a third party who is not under any obligation, whether contractual, fiduciary, statutory, or otherwise, of confidentiality to Disclosing Party with respect to su ch Confidential Information; (iv) are at any time developed by Receiving Party i ndependently without use of, or reference to, the Confidential Information of the other Party, or(v) are disclosed pursuant to the order or requirement of a court, administrative agency, or other governmental body with proper jurisdiction; provided, however, that Receiving Party uses diligent efforts to limit such disclosure, endeavors to obtain assurance that confidential treatment w ill be accorded the Confid ential Information so disclosed, and notifies Disclosing Party within five (5) days of receipt of such court order or requirement to enable Disclosing Party to seek a protective order or otherwise prevent or restrict such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means information deemed confidential by the Disclosing Party. Confidential Information shall be deemed to include all notes, analyses, loan documents, compilati ons and other material prepared by a recipient of Confidential Information containing or based in whole or in part on any Confidential Information. Confidential Information does not include information or materials that (i) are or became generally known or available to the public through no fault of Receiving Part y; (ii) were already known to Receiving Party without restriction, prior to receipt from Disclosing Party, as evidenced by files in existence at the time of such disclosure; (iii) are la wfully disclosed to Receiving Party by a third party who is not under any obligation, whether contractual, fiduciary, statutory, or otherwise, of confidentiality to Disclosing Party with respect to su ch Confidential Information; (iv) are at any time developed by Receiving Party i ndependently without use of, or reference to, the Confidential Information of the other Party, or(v) are disclosed pursuant to the order or requirement of a court, administrative agency, or other governmental body with proper jurisdiction; provided, however, that Receiving Party uses diligent efforts to limit such disclosure, endeavors to obtain assurance that confidential treatment w ill be accorded the Confid ential Information so disclosed, and notifies Disclosing Party within five (5) days of receipt of such court order or requirement to enable Disclosing Party to seek a protective order or otherwise prevent or restrict such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the detailed definition of 'Confidential Information' and replacing it with a vague statement like 'information deemed confidential by the Disclosing Party' creates an in-text contradiction. The subsequent clauses (a, b, c, d) which further clarify what constitutes confidential information are now meaningless or contradictory, as the primary determinant rests solely on the disclosing party's subjective judgment. This also makes the exceptions (i to v) ambiguous, since their applicability now depends on the disclosing party's initial determination.
perturbation.contradicted_text: “Confidential Information” means information deemed confidential by the Disclosing Party. Confidential Information shall be deemed to include all notes, analyses, loan documents, compilati ons and other material prepared by a recipient of Confidential Information containing or based in whole or in part on any Confidential Information. Confidential Information does not include information or materials that (i) are or became generally known or available to the public through no fault of Receiving Part y; (ii) were already known to Receiving Party without restriction, prior to receipt from Disclosing Party, as evidenced by files in existence at the time of such disclosure; (iii) are la wfully disclosed to Receiving Party by a third party who is not under any obligation, whether contractual, fiduciary, statutory, or otherwise, of confidentiality to Disclosing Party with respect to su ch Confidential Information; (iv) are at any time developed by Receiving Party i ndependently without use of, or reference to, the Confidential Information of the other Party, or(v) are disclosed pursuant to the order or requirement of a court, administrative agency, or other governmental body with proper jurisdiction; provided, however, that Receiving Party uses diligent efforts to limit such disclosure, endeavors to obtain assurance that confidential treatment w ill be accorded the Confid ential Information so disclosed, and notifies Disclosing Party within five (5) days of receipt of such court order or requirement to enable Disclosing Party to seek a protective order or otherwise prevent or restrict such disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a detailed definition of 'Confidential Information' with a vague statement, creating a contradiction. The contradicted text, containing clauses (i to v) clarifying the exceptions and inclusion of 'Confidential Information', becomes meaningless because the primary determinant of confidentiality now rests solely on the Disclosing Party's subjective judgement, contradicting the previous specific examples of what is and isn't considered confidential.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Confidential Information of either Party may not be copied or reproduced by the other Party without the disclosing Party’s prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Confidential Information of either Party may be copied or reproduced by the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause, `without the disclosing Party’s prior written consent.` Now Section 5 says that any party can copy the `Confidential Information of either Party may be copied or reproduced by the other Party`, which contradicts Section 10, which states that any disclosure would cause harm to any party, so if the information is now able to be copied or reproduced by either party, how would that be in agreement with preventing harm? The lack of limitations leads to the harm the agreement seeks to protect against.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. The Parties acknowledge and agree that irrepara ble harm may occur if any of the Confidential Information were to be disclosed to third parties or if any use were to be made of the Confidential Information other than that specified in this Agre ement, and the Parties further agree that each shall have the right to seek and obtain injunctive relief upon any violation or threatened violation of the terms of this Agreement, in add ition to all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that Confidential Information could not be copied or reproduced without the disclosing Party’s prior written consent. The changed text removes this restriction, now allowing either party to copy or reproduce the information. Section 10 states that irreparable harm may occur if any Confidential Information were to be disclosed or used other than as specified in the Agreement. By allowing either party to copy or reproduce the information without restriction, the changed text directly contradicts the purpose of Section 10, which is to prevent harm from disclosure or unauthorized use. This creates a strong contradiction, as the agreement seeks to protect against harm, yet now freely permits actions that could cause that harm. This aligns with the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition where the changed text creates a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 10. The Parties acknowledge and agree that irrepara ble harm may occur if any of the Confidential Information were to be disclosed to third parties or if any use were to be made of the Confidential Information other than that specified in this Agre ement, and the Parties further agree that each shall have the right to seek and obtain injunctive relief upon any violation or threatened violation of the terms of this Agreement, in add ition to all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. The Parties acknowledge and agree that irrepara ble harm may occur if any of the Confidential Information were to be disclosed to third parties or if any use were to be made of the Confidential Information other than that specified in this Agre ement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause ,`and the Parties further agree that each shall have the right to seek and obtain injunctive relief upon any violation or threatened violation of the terms of this Agreement, in add ition to all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity.`, the agreement lacks clarity on the appropriate methods if one party violates the terms. This creates an in-text contradiction because harm can occur, but no relief can come due to the lack of a defined method.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. The Parties acknowledge and agree that irrepara ble harm may occur if any of the Confidential Information were to be disclosed to third parties or if any use were to be made of the Confidential Information other than that specified in this Agre ement.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that parties have the right to seek injunctive relief for violations, while the changed text removes this right. The contradicted text acknowledges that irreparable harm may occur. The removal of the injunctive relief clause directly contradicts the ability to address the potential harm outlined in the contradicted text, creating a strong in-text contradiction because a remedy previously defined is now absent.
